coredata.dc:title: Roles of PTEN gene methylation in Se-CQDs induced mitochondrial apoptosis of osteosarcoma cells
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Huahua
Lv, Yan
Zhu, Lixian
Wang, Yanhua
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing anti-osteosarcoma elements are intriguing therapeutics promising for bioimaging and tumor therapy. However, how the anti-osteosarcoma element doped in the structure of such CQDs triggers tumor inhibition remains unclear. Here, selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) are developed via a one-step hydrothermal route using discarded orange peel as a carbon source and structurally characterized by various physicochemical techniques. The biocompatibility and anti-osteosarcoma efficacy are deeply evaluated using animal and cell models. The resulting spherical Se-CQDs, with a 3–7 nm diameter, possess green-yellow tunable luminescence and excellent biocompatibility. Cell experiments show that Se-CQDs can be up-taken by osteosarcoma U2OS cells and activate the mitochondrial apoptosis pathway triggered by increased reactive oxygen species. They can arrest the cell cycle at the G2/S phase and promote cellular apoptosis with reduced invasion and migration. Molecularly, Se-CQDs can down-regulate the expression of DNMT1 while up-regulating the expression of PTEN due to the decreased promoter methylation. Notably, Se-incorporated CQDs are more effective in inhibiting the proliferation, migration, and invasion of osteosarcoma than Se-free CQDs. It is feasible to use Se-CQDs as candidates for the potential application of early monitoring and treatment of osteosarcoma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Selenium-doped carbon quantum dots
Osteosarcoma
Mitochondria
Apoptosis
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of Se-CQDs and CQDs
Salt stability of Se-CQDs and release profile of Se ions
In vitro stability of Se-CQD and CQD in serum
In vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity
Se-CQDs promote apoptosis of U2OS cells
Se-CQDs inhibit migration and invasion of U2OS cells
Se-CQDs arrest cell cycle and impair the function of mitochondria
Se-CQDs regulate the expression of mitochondrial apoptosis related proteins
Se-CQDs promote the expression of DNA methylation-associated genes in U2OS cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy
Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy of China Three Gorges University
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to sincerely thank for the help from Dr. Chunyu Cao in the Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy. This work was financially supported by open research fund from The Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy of China Three Gorges University (grant no. 2022KZL2–01) and by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 81602559). All the authors in this study commented on previous versions of the manuscript. Also, they read and approved the final manuscript. Written informed consent was obtained from all healthy individuals who were included in the study. Informed consent was documented in a written, signed, and dated informed consent form.

The authors would like to sincerely thank for the help from Dr. Chunyu Cao in the Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy. This work was financially supported by open research fund from The Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy of China Three Gorges University (grant no. 2022KZL2–01 ) and by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 81602559 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Se-CQDs (a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of Se-CQDs; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of Se-CQDs (unnormalized and normalized fluorescence emission spectra); (c) TEM images of Se-CQDs; (d) SAED images of Se-CQDs; (e) a XPS wide-scan spectrum of Se-CQDs; (f) FTIR spectra of Se-CQDs and CQDs; (g) XRD spectra of CQDs and Se-CQDs.
Salt stability of Se-CQDs and element analysis. (a) Se ion release in PBS buffers; (b) Analysis of energy spectrum of Se-CQDs; (c) Elemental mapping of Se-CQDs.
The biosafety of Se-CQDs (a) Cytotoxicity of CQDs and Se-CQDs on HEK 293 T cells and BMSCs cells; (b) Serum Biochemical Index Analysis; (c) Images of H&E-stained organ samples.

Effects of Se-CQDs on viability of osteosarcoma cells (a) The distribution of Se-CQDs in osteosarcoma cells; (b) the effect of different concentrations of Se-CQDs on the viability of osteosarcoma cells; (c) Morphology of cells in each group under light microscope; (d) Cell apoptosis detection in each group using flow cytometry; Compare with Control group, * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01.

The influence of Se-CQDs on U2OS cell migration and invasion (a) Transwell assay for cell migration and quantification of migrated cells; (b) Transwell assay for cell invasion and quantification of invasion cells; (c) Wound-healing assay and wound-healing rates; (d) Western blot is used to detect the protein expressions of Annexin A2, MMP-9 and MMP-2 in each group.

The influence of Se-CQDs on cell cycle of U2OS cell, ROS, mitochondrial membrane potentials and cellular proliferation. (a) Phase distribution in cell cycle and the bar diagram of the average percentage of cells in different phases of the cell cycle in each group; (b) Western blot is used to detect the protein expressions of Cyclin B1 and CDC2 in each group; (c) The production of ROS in each group; (d) Changes of mitochondrial membrane potentials (MMPs) in each group.
Western blotting is used to measure the expression level of apoptosis-related proteins in U2OS cells.

Western blotting and RT-qPCR are used to evaluate the expression of methylation-associated genes such as PTEN, APC and DNMT1. (a) the bands of Western blot; (b) the data analysis of each group from Western blot; (c) RT-qPCR analysis. The data are analyzed statistically using analysis of variance (ANOVA). ** P < 0.01 vs control group, # P < 0.05 vs CQDs group, ## P < 0.01 vs CQDs group.

head.ce:title: Roles of PTEN gene methylation in Se-CQDs induced mitochondrial apoptosis of osteosarcoma cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Life Science Building, China Three Gorges University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatible carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing anti-osteosarcoma elements are intriguing therapeutics promising for bioimaging and tumor therapy. However, how the anti-osteosarcoma element doped in the structure of such CQDs triggers tumor inhibition remains unclear. Here, selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) are developed via a one-step hydrothermal route using discarded orange peel as a carbon source and structurally characterized by various physicochemical techniques. The biocompatibility and anti-osteosarcoma efficacy are deeply evaluated using animal and cell models. The resulting spherical Se-CQDs, with a 3–7 nm diameter, possess green-yellow tunable luminescence and excellent biocompatibility. Cell experiments show that Se-CQDs can be up-taken by osteosarcoma U2OS cells and activate the mitochondrial apoptosis pathway triggered by increased reactive oxygen species. They can arrest the cell cycle at the G2/S phase and promote cellular apoptosis with reduced invasion and migration. Molecularly, Se-CQDs can down-regulate the expression of DNMT1 while up-regulating the expression of PTEN due to the decreased promoter methylation. Notably, Se-incorporated CQDs are more effective in inhibiting the proliferation, migration, and invasion of osteosarcoma than Se-free CQDs. It is feasible to use Se-CQDs as candidates for the potential application of early monitoring and treatment of osteosarcoma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignancies in bone, which usually occurs in children and adolescents, and this malignant neoplasm greatly destroys the normal structure of the distal femur and proximal tibia [1–3]. Current treatment options for osteosarcoma include surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy [2,4]. However, these approaches do not tremendously prolong patients’ survival, especially for individuals with metastatic osteosarcoma [5–7]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to seek new therapeutic strategies to control the development of tumors.

As a carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely investigated for biomedical applications because of their luminescence, small size, good dispensability, and low bio-toxicity [8–10]. However, CQDs prepared by conventional methods exhibit low fluorescence quantum yields and dismal hydrophilicity, which significantly limits the scope of their application. Luckily, growing efforts show that modifying the physical and chemical properties via altering constituent elements and the internal structure of CQDs could help expand the application in tumor therapy.

Selenium (Se) is a trace mineral element in bone that maintains bone health and structural remodeling [11,12]. It is shown that Se performs excellent anti-cancer, anti-oxidation, and anti-bacterial effects due to the nature of the element itself [13–15]. Factually, recent investigations about Se-CQDs have mostly focused on free radical scavenging or fluorescence diagnosis [16–21]. Fewer studies have focused on the biological effects and molecular mechanisms of Se-CQDs in osteosarcoma therapy. Se metabolic pool exhibits organic or inorganic Se species metabolized by methylation in the human body. Also, our previous research on Se-doped hydroxyapatite nano-crystals showed that Se could down-regulate the promoter methylation of the PTEN gene in osteosarcoma SOSP-9607 cells. Whether Se-doped carbon-dot materials have the same epigenetic regulatory effects has yet to be fully understood. Here, Se-CQDs are green synthesized utilizing discarded orange peel through a hydrothermal route and structurally characterized by physicochemical techniques. Human osteosarcoma SOSP-9607, U2OS cell model and Kunming mouse model are applied to evaluate anti-osteosarcoma efficacy and biocompatibility. Importantly, the correlation between mitochondrial apoptosis signaling and methylation change of tumor suppressor genes such as PTEN and APC are also investigated.

For the greener synthesis, the source of Na2SeO3 (Micxy Reagent Company, China) and carbon (made from discarded orange peel) are ground to a fine powder and stored in a vacuum-drying oven. The chemicals such as NaOH, HCl, absolute ethanol, and neutral resin are purchased from Sinopharm (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and DMEM medium are obtained from Procell Company (China). The detection kits for ALT, AST, BUN, Cr, ALP, and LDH are purchased from Nanjing Jianchen Institute of Bioengineering, China. In addition, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) kits are provided from Wanleibio (China). Furthermore, the primary antibodies of β-actin (Servicebio, China), Annexin A2 and CDC2 (Cell Signaling Technology, USA), MMP-9, MMP-2, Cyclin B1, Bcl-2, Bax, Cytochrome C (Cyto-C), Caspase-3, Cleaved Caspase-3, PARP, Cleaved PARP, Caspase-9, Cleaved Caspase-9, c-Myc, PTEN, DNMT1, APC and P53 (Wanleibio, China) are used in the analysis of Western blot. The anti-rabbit secondary antibodies are purchased from Servicebio (China). Human osteosarcoma cells SOSP-9607 are donated by the Bone Cancer Institute of the Fourth Military Medical University, China, and another cell line, U2OS, is purchased from Wuhan Procell Biological Company. Human embryonic kidney cells (HEK 293 T) and bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) are purchased from Wuhan University Collection Center. Cells are cultured in a high-glucose DMEM medium containing 10% FBS in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 ℃ with medium changes every 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Se-CQDs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The crystals of Se-CQDs are synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal route (a detailed synthetic scheme is summarized in Supplementary material Fig. S1). Briefly, for Se-CQDs preparation, 2 g of orange peel powder and 0.4 g of Na2SeO3 are dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and mixed well at room temperature to get the Se-C precursor solution. For pure CQDs, just 2 g of orange peel powder is dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water to get C precursor solution. After that, this precursor solution is transferred to a PTFE-lined reactor and heated at 180 ℃ for 5 h in an oven. The resultant solution is centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min and filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 µm), then it is purified by dialysis using a dialysis bag with 1000 molecular weight cut-off for 48 h. After final freeze-drying, the products of Se-CQDs or CQDs are stored at 4 ℃ for further analysis.

The luminescent property of as-prepared Se-CQDs is examined by UV-Vis equipment (UV-1900, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of Se-CQDs are measured on an RF-600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The morphology of Se-CQDs is detected by transmission electron microscopy (JEOLJEM-2100 F, TEM) accompanied by selected area electron microscopy diffraction (SAED) measurements. The element and valence contained in the final product are detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (Escalab250Xi spectrometer). The crystal structure of Se-CQDs is characterized by a DX-2700BH X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The functional groups in the Se-CQDs are measured via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra using the KBr tablet on FTS-25PC Fourier infrared spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt stability of Se-CQDs and release profile of Se ions
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the salt stability, 50 mg of Se-CQDs powder is immersed in 10 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH7.2 ± 0.2) and maintained at 37 ℃ waterbath with continuous stirring. At each pre-determinant time interval, 1 mL of the supernatant is withdrawn by centrifugation and replaced by the same volume of fresh medium. If Se-CQDs can be degraded in PBS salt, it may release Se ions into the supernatant. Then, the amount of Se ions in the supernatant can be measured by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Nanoquant infinite M200 PRO, Tecan) at a wavelength of 365 nm and calculated using a standard calibration curve obtained under the same conditions. All the tests are performed in triplicate, and the average values are present in this study.

To detect whether Se-CQDs and CQDs can cause hemolysis, the whole blood of thirty healthy individuals is collected from the first college of clinical medical science of China Three Gorges University, and confirmed in the normalcy via blood-routine test. Briefly, 10 mg of each sample is resolved in 10 mL PBS (pH7.2) solution and ultrasonic dispersed for 15 min, then 20 μL of the above sample solution is added into 1 mL of human whole blood. After that, the blood solution is incubated in 37 ℃ for 15 min, and finally, the changes of the whole blood are monitored via blood routine data obtained from blood-cell-analysis equipment (Mindray BC-1800, China). The increase of the Hemoglubin quantity in the blood routine data could reflect the strong hemolytic toxicity of the biomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability of Se-CQD and CQD in serum
ce:section.ce:para: According to the methods reported in the literature [22], the stability of Se-CQD and CQD in serum is investigated by measuring the mixture turbidity changes of Se-CQD and CQD in 50% feotal bovine serum (FBS) system. Briefly, different concentrations (10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, 60 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL) of Se-CQD and CQD in HEPES buffer solution are mixed with equal volume of 50% FBS. Then, the mixtures are incubated at 37 ℃ with 60 rpm shaking, and 200 μL of sample is collected at each predetermined time point (0, 3, 7, 20, 27 and 30 h). Finally, the transmittance of each sample at 750 nm is measured by a microplate reader (infinite 200Pro, Tecan). For analysis, HEPES buffer containing 50% FBS is designed as blank control, while Se-CQD or CQD mixed with equal volume of HEPES buffer is used as negative control for Se-CQD or CQD samples, respectively.

MTT assay is used to detect the toxicity of Se-CQDs to normal cells. Briefly, cells (1 ×105 cells/well) are inoculated into 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h in an incubator. Then, 1.0 mg/mL of CQDs and different concentrations (0.25–2.5 mg/mL) of Se-CQDs are added to the culture system. When cells co-incubate with materials for 24 or 48 h, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) is added to the culture media. After four hours of reaction, 150 μL of DMSO is added to each well to dissolve methane fully. Finally, the absorbance values (i.e. OD) of the wells are measured at 570 nm by a microplate reader, and the following equation calculates cell viability: [OD]e/ [OD]c× 100%, where [OD]e is the OD value of experimental group, [OD]c is the OD value from non-treated control group.

Cell migration and invasion are detected by Wound healing and Transwell test. In the wound-healing experiment, the confluent monolayer of U2OS cells is damaged with a 200 μL yellow pipette tip. Then, the detached cells are discarded by washing twice with PBS. After that, the remnant attached cells are co-cultured with 1 mg/mL of Se-CQDs. The migration of cells from one side of the injury edge to the other is recorded by light microscope at 0 h, 24 h and 48 h. Image J software is used to analyze and calculate the scratch area. In the Transwell assay, Matrigel is diluted at the ratio of 1:8 using distilled water, and then 60 μL of diluted matrix gel is added to the upper chamber of the Transwell and placed in the incubator for 4–5 h before removal. Approximately 1 × 104 cells in serum-free medium are inoculated into the upper chamber precoated with Matrigel, and these cells are treated by Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL). In contrast, 500 μL of medium containing 10% FBS is added to the lower chamber and then incubated for 24 h in the incubator. Before detection, the medium in the Transwell chamber is completely discarded, washed thrice with PBS, fixed in methanol for 20 min, and then stained with crystal violet for 20 min. The invasive cells are photographed and counted under the microscope in random multiple views. Matrigel-coated basement membranes are not required for the Transwell migration assay, and the other steps are the same as for the invasion assay.

Cell cycle distribution is measured using a cell cycle assay kit. After Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) are co-cultured with U2OS cells for 48 h, the cells are collected and fixed with 70% cold ethanol at 4 ℃ overnight. After that, these cells are reacted with 500 μL of PI/RNase (9:1) working solution. Finally, the cell content is determined by flow cytometry and analyzed using FCS Express version 6 software.

The ROS generation in U2OS cells is measured by fluorescence of 20,70-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) originated from DCFH-DA using flow cytometry. The cells were collected after Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) or CQDs (1 mg/mL) interacted with U2OS cells for 48 h. DCFH-DA at a concentration of 10 μM is added to the designated treated cells which are then incubated in an incubator for 30 min. Cells are washed twice with a serum-free medium to wash away DCFH-DA that does not enter the cells. Then, the level of ROS is measured by the fluorescence intensity (FL-1, 530 nm) of 10000 cells with flow cytometry. The mean fluorescence intensities are calculated by histogram statistics using the cytometer CXP software.

To probe the changes of mitochondrial transmembrane potential after being treated with Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) or CQDs (1 mg/mL) for 48 h, rhodamine 123, a kind of cellular permeable cationic dye, is used to stain the U2OS cells. Often, 10 μL of staining solution (rhodamine 123, 5 μg/mL) is added to the indicated treated cells cultured in the cell incubator for 10 min. After that, the cells are washed once with fresh medium and then replaced with a new one, and continue the culture for 60 min in the incubator. Finally, the alteration of the mitochondrial membrane potential is measured by the fluorescence intensity (ex, 488–505 nm; em, 530 nm) with flow cytometry. For each experiment, 5000 events per sample are recorded.

The Annexin V-FITC/PI double stain assay is conducted to detect apoptosis. After co-cultured with Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) or CQDs (1 mg/mL) for 48 h, 5 mL of Annexin V-FITC and 5 mL of PI are added to the culture medium and then further incubated in the dark for 15 min. After that, each sample is immediately analyzed with flow cytometry (Beckman Coulter Cytomics FC500, USA). The detection is conducted at excitation wave (488 nm), and the emitted green fluorescence of Annexin V and red fluorescence of PI is collected using 525 and 575 nm band pass filters, respectively. The test is performed thrice, and the apoptosis percentage in each group is calculated.

A laser confocal microscope is utilized to observe whether Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) can be up-taken by U2OS cells and emit fluorescence. Briefly, U2OS cells are inoculated into confocal culture dishes at a density of 1000 cells per well, and Se-CQDs at a mass concentration of 1 mg/mL are added to the culture system when the cells have grown to about 80% of the bottom area of the dish. Finally, the culture dish is checked and photographed under a confocal microscope.

To further detect the expression level of methylation-related genes such as PTEN, DNMT1, APC, and mitochondrial apoptosis-related protein, Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) or CQDs (1 mg/mL) are incubated with U2OS cells for 48 h, then the total cell protein is extracted using RIPA protein lysate, and the protein is quantified by the BCA method. 30 μg of cellular protein is used for Western blotting analysis, which follows previously established protocols with slight modifications [23]. Briefly, different masses of the proteins are separated by 10% SDS-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. After electrophoresis, the protein-containing SDS-polyacrylamide gel is transferred to the PVDF membrane. The PVDF membranes are blocked in TBST containing 5% skim milk for 1 h. The primary antibody is diluted by TBST solution at a volume ratio of 1:1000 and cold-incubated in refrigerator overnight at 4 ℃. After that, the secondary antibody that TBST dilutes at a volume ratio of 1:2000 is added into the detection system and then incubating at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, the PVDF membrane is washed by TBST and applied for detection. The gray value of each band is processed by Image J software, and the protein expression is expressed as the ratio of the target band to the internal reference (β-actin) band.

The mRNA expression levels of DNA methylation-related genes, including DNMT1 and PTEN, are further investigated by RT-qPCR. The U2OS cells are co-cultured with CQDs (1 mg/mL) or Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) for 48 h, followed by the isolation of total RNA using the RNA simple Total Kit (TianGen, China). The RT-qPCR reaction is used to detect the expression level of DNMT1 and PTEN by SYBR Premix Ex Taq and the light cycler 96 system (Roche, Germany). GAPDH serves as an internal reference gene. Fold changes in gene expression are calculated by the 2−ΔΔCT method. The primer sequence of each gene is provided in (Supplementary material Table 1).

60 male Kunming (KM) mice of SPF grade are purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of China Three Gorges University. The initial body weight of the experimental mice is 18–22 g. All experiments are carried out under the approval of the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of China Three Gorges University, and the license number for the use of experimental animal facilities is SYXK (E) 2017–0061. In detail, sixty male Kunming mice are randomly divided into two large groups (a 1-day group for detecting acute toxicity and a 28-day group for detecting cumulative toxicity; n = 30 in each group). For detection, each group is divided into three subgroups (n = 10 in each subgroup): The control group, the CQDs group, and the Se-CQDs group. The animals in the control group (n = 10) are given an appropriate amount of normal saline by intraperitoneal injection once a day for 1 or 28 days. The animals in CQDs (n = 10) and Se-CQDs groups (n = 10) are administered at 10 mg/kg daily via intraperitoneal injection for 1 or 28 days. At the end of the intervention, all mice are sacrificed, and blood is collected for serum biochemical assays and whole- blood testing. Major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) of each group are collected after continuous intraperitoneal injection for 28 days. The samples are stained with HE, observed under a microscope, and comparatively analyzed.

All experiments in this study are repeated at least 3 times. The experimental results are analyzed and plotted using the statistical software GraphPad Prism 9, and the measurement data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation
x
̅
± SD
. The means of two samples are compared t-test, and One-way ANOVA is used to compare multiple groups, P < 0.05 indicates a statistically significant difference.

The Se-CQDs prepared by the hydrothermal method disperse uniformly in water without agglomeration. As shown in Fig. 1, the Se-CQDs solution is brownish-yellow under UV light (365 nm). It emits yellow fluorescence visible to the naked eye, indicating excellent photoluminescence properties. The UV absorption spectrum shows that Se-CQDs have strong absorption in the UV region and a characteristic absorption peak situated at 272.5 nm, which may be attributed to the CC bond π→π * on Se-CQDs produced by transition absorption (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 1 b) shows that the fluorescence emission of CQDs is wavelength-dependent. Interestingly, the emission wavelength of C-dots gradually presents red-shifts with increased excitation wavelength in the 340–480 nm range, implying the typical "fluorescence red-shift phenomenon" of C-dots. The TEM image (Fig. 1 c) shows that the average particle size of the synthesized Se-CQDs is about 3–7 nm, and the interplanar spacing of the Se-CQDs is approximately 0.244 nm. The SAED image (Fig. 1 d) shows that the crystal planes are regular hexagons. XPS spectra (Fig. 1 e) showed that significant Se3d signals are observed in the Se3d spectra of Se-CQDs, indicating that Se is successfully introduced into the structure of CQDs via forming Se-O bonds. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 f) shows that a broad O-H stretching vibration peak is situated at 3370 cm−1, and a distinctive peak at 2940 cm−1 represents C-H stretching and bending vibrations. The stretching vibration centered at 1516 cm−1 is attributed to C-C group. The peak located at 1638 cm−1 represents the amide stretching vibration of CO. In addition, the stretching vibration peaks centered at 1040 cm−1 are attributed mostly to carboxyl group (C-O bond). It is confirmed that the synthesized Se-CQDs possess a variety of organic functional groups, and these oxygen-containing groups enable Se-CQDs to have good solubility in water. Se-CQDs are very similar to those of CQDs, and their surfaces are rich in hydroxyl groups, favoring good hydrophilicity and stability. Additionally, the XRD pattern (Fig. 1 g) shows that the Se-CQDs have a single broad diffraction peak centered at 22.72°, attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with amorphous properties. When the amount of doped Se is 0.1 g in the reaction material, the content of Se in the synthesized Se-CQDs product is about (0.02449 ± 0.00008) g, which XRF detects. The effective doping ratio of Se in the resulting Se-CQDs is (24.49 ± 0.08)% (Supplementary material Table 2). Along with the introduction of Se into the carbon dots, the content of Ca decreases gradually in the product. Therefore, the Se-CQDs could be successfully prepared through greener synthesis via the hydrothermal route.

The Se-CQDs are partly dissolved in phosphate buffer (pH7.2 ± 0.2), gradually releasing Se ions. As shown in Fig. 2a, few Se ions can be detected in the supernatant. At about 20 h, only 3% of Se ions are present in such a solution. However, until 118 h, the released Se ions are no more than 17%. The amount of Se released from such material seems linearly related to its immersing time. Reasonably, Se-CQDs are not very stable in PBS salt. For the reason that these salt buffers can degrade it. Interestingly, the analysis of elemental mapping and energy spectrum demonstrated that, except for Se, other elements such as Ca, P, K, C, O, etc, were also involved in the final product of Se-CQDs (Fig. 2b&c), mainly caused by the natural components of orange peel. Such elements are attributed to the increased solubility of the product. As such, Se-CQDs are evidenced by some oxygen-containing groups, and hydroxyl groups are involved in their chemical structure. Surprisingly, the release of Se ions is closely related to the solubility, final content and Se-O state in CQDs. Although there are more oxygen-containing and hydroxyl groups in Se-CQDs material, and its solubility is good, the total amount of Se contained in such material is low, leading to the total amount of Se released not being high.

Factually, previous research hinted the hemolysis less than 10% was always deemed as biocompatible [24]. Surprisingly, CQDs and Se-CQDs have no hemolysis properties in human blood (Supplementary material Table 3). Statistically, there is no difference between CQDs and Se-CQDs, which indicates the C-dots materials derived from the orange peel exhibited almost no toxicity to human blood cells, and it could hardly cause hemolysis of the red blood cells (RBC) because the content of the hemoglobin was in the normal range of the healthy individuals. Also, other parameters, such as WBC, PLT, HCT, etc, presented no significant difference between the CQDs and Se-CQDs group. It is demonstrated that the synthetic Se-CQDs are hemo-compatibility with human blood cells.

The stability of Se-CQD and CQD is tested in 50% FBS. As illustrated in Supplementary material Fig. S2a, five different concentrations of CQD are relatively unstable since the transmittance shows increased change in HEPES buffer containing 50% FBS over 30 h. This might be attributed to surface interaction between cationic surface of CQD and negative charge of serum proteins. Interestingly, after the introduction of Se ions into CQD (Se-CQD sample), the transmittance decreases first and then increases with the extension of time (Supplementary material Fig. S2b). It is possible that, the increased hydrophilicity and solubility due to ion doping benefit the facile degradation of Se-CQD in serum, and this leads to the decreased transmittance within 0–20 h. However, it is no statistical changes among different concentrations from 20 h to 27 h, and it seems that 20–27 h is the turning point at which this sample stabilizes in the serum. More importantly, after 27 h, the increased transmittance is recorded due to the serum proteins easily binding to the released Se ions and C-dots caused by materials degradation and electrostatic attraction. In contrast, CQD and Se-CQD present completely different models in serum (Supplementary material Fig. S2c). The addition of Se ions into CQD considerably increases the rate of decomposition in serum, especially in the initial 20 h, whereas Se-free CQD improves the adhesion rate of serum protein. Certain reports also provide some explanation, different surface charge or even different size might exhibit various influence on the stability of serum. For example, opsonins in serum are more favorably binding to positively charged nanoparticles, and size larger than 200 nm can activate the complement proteins [25]. Likewise, serum samples of different species presumably bring significant changes to nanoparticles, such as size increase, surface charge neutralization and blocked ligands. As reported by Joaqui-Joaqui, DNA nanostructures exhibited species-specific stability in different media [26]. Predictively, Se-CQD in the study is a highly active nano-material with better biocompatibility and serum degradability, whereas more research is needed to confirm these properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: After co-culturing with Se-CQDs, the density and viability of HEK 293 T cells and BMSCs cells do not change significantly. As shown in Fig. 3 a, when co-incubated with Se-CQDs for 24 h or 48 h, the cell viability of both HEK 293 T cells and BMSCs cells is about 90%, indicating that the Se-CQDs have negligible cytotoxicity to such normal cells. Next, the KM mouse model is used to assess the toxicity of Se-CQDs in vivo. On the 28th day after the injection of CQDs and Se-CQDs, the eyeball blood is sampled for the blood routine test. The data of the whole blood test (Supplementary material Table 4) present no significant difference among the groups, suggesting that CQDs and Se-CQDs possess negligible toxicity to the whole blood of KM mice. Similarly, the biochemistry analysis for the parameters such as ALT, AST, ALP, TBIL, LDH, BUN, and CRE reveals there are no obvious abnormalities in the important organs after being treated by Se-CQDs (Fig. 3 b). Notably, the histopathological examination showed that the histologic structure of five important organs (heart/liver/spleen/lung/kidney) is wholly the same as the control group, whether it is tested on day 1 or on day 28. It is proven that Se-CQDs present almost no organic damage. Surprisingly, after continuous injection of Se-CQDs for 28 days, slight hepatocyte edema (reversible damage) is found in the slide of the liver. Fortunately, after injection termination for 2 days, the histologic structure of the liver returned to its original morphology (Fig. 3 c), indicating that Se-CQDs have good histocompatibility and no significant organ toxicity to these mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se-CQDs promote apoptosis of U2OS cells
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs commonly enter cells through endocytosis [27]. Due to the fluorescent properties of Se-CQDs, it is facile to capture green fluorescence under excitation at 488 nm. Once these particles co-incubate with osteosarcoma cells, they can be phagocytosed by osteosarcoma cells and distribute evenly near the nucleus checked under laser confocal microscopy. It is found that a dense distribution of yellow-green fluorescence is seen around the cytoplasm and nucleus over time ( Fig. 4 a). Factually, at the very beginning, the Se-CQDs aggregate near the cell membrane, but after 30 min of co-culture, they appear in the cytoplasm, and about one hour later, it is easy to observe the entry of these particles to the nucleus. It is indicated that these tumor cells perform strong phagocytosis on the Se-CQDs particles.

The Se-CQD particles entering the cells can also significantly inhibit the growth of osteosarcoma cells. In a preliminary experiment, it is evident that 1.00 mg/mL of Se-CQDs reduced cell viability by nearly 60% in osteosarcoma SOSP-9607 cells, and the effect is stronger than the same concentration of CQDs (Supplementary material Fig. S2). Likewise, Se-CQDs inhibit the proliferation of U2OS cells in a dose-dependent manner, and the inhibitory effect of Se-free CQDs on U2OS cells is weaker than that of Se-CQDs (Fig. 4 b). When U2OS cells are co-cultured with 1.00 mg/mL of Se-CQDs for 48 h, almost half of the cells are dead, testifying IC50 value of Se-CQDs is 1.00 mg/mL. Differently, under the same conditions, cell growth is seldomly affected in the CQDs group, and the cells adhere well to the culture plate. Conversely, the number of cells in the Se-CQDs group decreases significantly, the cells present pyknosis, and the intercellular space enlarges, certain cells even fall off in the form of cell fragments (Fig. 4 c). Likewise, the cell apoptosis rate reaches (9.93 ± 1.33)% in Se-CQDs group which is significantly higher than that in control group (Fig. 4 d). It is proven that Se-CQDs could significantly inhibit the proliferation of osteosarcoma cells, and its inhibitory effect is stronger than that of CQDs without Se, indicating that the incorporation of Se could enhance the anticancer properties of CQDs. Consistent with our previous research about Se-doped hydroxyapatite, Se plays a crucial role in anti-osteosarcoma [28]. Recent evidence also showed that, as different from normal cells, cancer cells could contain more Se ions in the cytoplasm due to the high concentration of thiols, while healthy cells have relative resistance to selenium concentration, which may be the mechanism that leads to the facilitated entry of selenium-containing materials into cancer cells to play an anticancer role [29–31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se-CQDs inhibit migration and invasion of U2OS cells
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential of Se-CQDs to inhibit migration and invasion of U2OS cells, we performed Transwell and wound healing assays on U2OS cells. The results ( Fig. 5 a&b) showed the changes in the number of U2OS cells that passed through the polycarbonate membrane pores of the Transwell chamber. It is evident that the smallest number of passed cells is found in the Se-CQDs group, but the largest number of cells is in the Control group, implying that Se-CQDs present the strongest anti-migration ability. Likewise, the wound healing assay (Fig. 5 c) shows that compared with the control and CQDs groups, it is notable that a lower wound healing rate is found in the Se-CQDs group. Strikingly, the results from Western blotting showed that the expression of MMP-9, MMP-2 and Annexin A2 protein in the Se-CQDs group was significantly lower than that in the control group. Comparatively, the expression of MMP-2 protein in the CQDs group was significantly lower than in the control group. Additionally, the expression of Annexin A2 protein was greatly reduced in such a group. It is suggested that Se-CQDs could significantly reduce the expression of Annexin A2, MMP-9, and MMP-2 proteins in osteosarcoma cells, further inhibiting the migration and invasion of osteosarcoma cells (Fig. 5 d). Commonly, osteosarcoma cells have invasive and metastatic potentials, which is a major cause of treatment failure [32]. Several studies have also shown that Matrix Metalloproteinases (MMPs) and Annexin A2 play a crucial role in tumor invasion and metastasis and are abundantly expressed in various malignant tumors [33–35]. Among MMPs, MMP-2 and MMP-9 are critical for initiating migration and invasion [36], [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se-CQDs arrest cell cycle and impair the function of mitochondria
ce:section.ce:para: Cell cycle arrest is one of the key mechanisms of antitumor activity, which inhibits cancer cell growth, induces apoptosis, and blocks cell cycle progression [38,39]. The data from flow cytometric detection on the cell cycle are summarized in Fig. 6 a. After incubated with Se-CQDs for 48 h, about (17.11 ± 7.50)% of U2OS cells are in the G0/G1 phase of the cell cycle, denoting a decrease of about 47% compared to the control group. In contrast, the percentage of the G2/M phase is significantly higher, calculating an increase of about 31.05% compared to the control group. Strikingly, the percentage of the G2/M phase in the Se-CQDs group increased significantly, counting about 31.05% compared to the control group. Likewise, it is evidenced from western blot (Fig. 6 b) that the expression of Cyclin B1 in the Se-CQDs group is significantly lower than that in the control group, and the expression of CDC2 protein is decreased compared to both the control and CQDs groups. The above results indicate that Se-CQDs can block the U2OS cell cycle of osteosarcoma cells at the G2/M phase.

To evaluate whether the treatment of the material can stimulate ROS production and thus promote apoptosis, U2OS cells are co-cultured with Se-CQDs (1 mg/mL) for 48 h. The ROS level in these cells is tested by flow cytometry using the ROS fluorescent probe DCFH-DA (Fig. 6 c). It is found that the level of ROS in U2OS cells treated with Se-CQDs is significantly increased. The increased amount is significantly higher than that in the CQDs group, proving that Se can irritate the generation of ROS. It is well known that when ROS production exceeds a threshold, MMPs (mitochondrial membrane potentials) will be deregulated, and these excessive ROS will impair mitochondrial function. Strikingly, the recorded MMPs in the Se-CQDs group are significantly lower than that in the control and CQDs group (Fig. 6 d). Theoretically, Se-CQDs may perform a damaging effect on MMPs, thus disrupting mitochondrial structure and function in U2OS cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se-CQDs regulate the expression of mitochondrial apoptosis related proteins
ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondrial dysfunction affects various interconnected cellular pathways, resulting in damage to intracellular components and the release of Cyto-C. After Cyto-C enters the cytoplasm, the Caspase cascade is triggered, activating the mitochondrial apoptosis pathway, leading to cellular apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 7, compared with the control group, the expression of the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 in the Se-CQDs group is decreased, and the expression level of Bax and Cyto-C in the cytoplasm is increased, further indicating that Se-CQDs can induce mitochondrial dysfunction. In addition, the expression of Caspase-9, Caspase-3 and PARP is significantly up-regulated, and the expression of Cleaved caspase-9, Cleaved caspase-3, and Cleaved PARP is accordingly increased. Importantly, the expression of the proto-oncogene c-Myc is reduced, indicating that Se-CQDs can inhibit the growth of osteosarcoma cells by activating the mitochondrial apoptosis pathways. Interestingly, the introduction of Se into the structure of CDQs could increase the protein expression of Bax, Caspase-3, Cleaved caspase-3, P53, Cyto-C and decrease the expression of c-Myc in U2OS cells compared to the pure CQDs. It is inferred that the introduction of Se doping could enhance the ability of CQDs to induce cellular apoptosis. Therefore, it is reasonable that the presence of Se exhibits a strong anti-osteosarcoma effect, closely related to the structure and properties of the Se atom itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se-CQDs promote the expression of DNA methylation-associated genes in U2OS cells
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8 a&b, it is evident that the tumor suppressor genes, including PTEN and APC, are up-regulated after being treated by Se-CQDs. However, in contrast with the control and CQDs group, the expression of DNMT1 is down-regulated in Se-CQDs. RT-qPCR is applied to detect the expression changes further to verify the mRNA level of DNMT1 and PTEN. Strikingly, the expression level of DNMT1 is dramatically decreased after being treated with Se-CQDs. Conversely, the expression level of PTEN is greatly promoted in the group of Se-CQDs (Fig. 8 c). As known, the promoter of the PTEN gene is highly methylated in osteosarcoma cells, which are tightly regulated by DNMT enzymes [40]. Furthermore, DNA methylation can silence the expression of PTEN, which accelerates the growth of tumor cells. Surprisingly, the results showed a decreased expression of DNMT1 while an increased expression of PTEN appeared in the Se-CQDs group. It is reasonable to infer the methylation of the PTEN gene is inhibited by Se-CQDs via the reduced DNMT1 expression. Presumably, Se-CQDs possess the function of demethylation activity for PTEN gene, which can finally inhibit the proliferation of U2OS cells.

Notably, there is a close correlation between the DNA methylation-related genes and the mitochondrial apoptosis genes. Se-CQDs could down-regulate the expression of DNMT1 in U2OS cells, inhibiting the promoter methylation of the PTEN gene. Also, the demethylation of the PTEN gene can promote its expression and subsequently activate mitochondrial signaling, accelerating the apoptosis of tumor cells. For osteosarcoma cells, the hyper-methylation of the PTEN gene promotes the proliferation and metastasis of tumor cells [40]. Strikingly, Se-CQDs present demethylation activity on the PTEN gene via the reduced DNMT1 activity, inhibiting the development of osteosarcoma. However, the interaction between the PTEN protein and the mitochondrial apoptosis pathway needs further study.

In this study, Se-doped carbon quantum dots (Se-CQDs) are successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. They present good biosafety in vitro and in vivo. They perform relatively high bioactivity with serum protein. They show almost no damage to the structure and function of major organs of KM mice. Se-CQDs can be up-taken via phagocytosis by U2OS cells, and they can strongly inhibit the proliferation, migration, and invasion of these cells. It is likely that the possible mechanism is related to the arrest of the cell cycle, which induces cell apoptosis via the mitochondria-apoptosis pathway. This effect is mainly attributed to the demethylation of the PTEN gene mediated by the decreased DNMT1 activity. As a novel type of nanomaterials, Se-CQDs have great application value in the early monitoring and treatment of osteosarcoma.

However, there are some limitations in the study. Firstly, the upstream molecular and gene regulatory mechanisms of the increased ROS production need further study. Furthermore, more animal experiments are needed to validate the in vivo anti-osteosarcoma mechanism of action of Se-CQDs. Nevertheless, whether the utility of such material presents similar anti-tumor effects in other types of neoplasms necessitates further exploration. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the preliminary work of this experiment has led to the surprising finding that it may have a regulatory effect on the methylation of certain tumor suppressor genes. It is promising that the evidence from an in-depth study of this material which focus on methylation regulatory genes, immunomodulatory molecules, and mitochondria function, will provide a more experimental basis for the clinical application of Se-CQDs.

This work synthesizes a new Se-CQDs material from orange peel through a simple, controlled, and eco-friendly method. Se-CQDs present spherical morphology with an average size of around 3–7 nm. Additionally, the results from XPS, FTIR, UV–vis, and FL techniques indicate that Se-CQDs are rich in hydroxyl groups on the surface, which results in good hydrophilicity and photoluminescence. Notably, when Se-CQDs are co-incubated with osteosarcoma cells for 30 min, green fluorescence is captured in the cytoplasm and almost in the periphery of the nucleus. When applied in vivo, Se-CQDs exhibit good hemo- and histo-compatibility in KM mice, presenting great potential as bioimaging probes. Interestingly, Se-CQDs inhibit the growth of osteosarcoma cells but have a negligible effect on normal cells. Mechanistically, Se-CQDs could activate the Cyto-C-mediated mitochondrial apoptosis pathway, further inhibiting the survival and proliferation of osteosarcoma cells while decreasing the potential of migration and invasion. Molecularly, Se-CQDs present demethylation activity on the PTEN gene via the reduced DNMT1 activity, promoting the mitochondrial apoptosis of osteosarcoma cells. Therefore, this environmentally friendly and simple-fast synthetic route for fabricating Se-CQDs offers great potential and versatility in the early monitoring and treatment of osteosarcoma.
This study was confirmed by Medical Ethics Committee of China Three Gorges University (No.2020030A) and all experiments were performed in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations.
Zhu Lixian: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lv Yan: Writing – review & editing. Su Huahua: Methodology. Wang Yanhua: Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to sincerely thank for the help from Dr. Chunyu Cao in the Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy. This work was financially supported by open research fund from The Hubei Key Laboratory of Tumor Microenvironment and Immunotherapy of China Three Gorges University (grant no. 2022KZL2–01) and by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 81602559).

ce:section.ce:para: All the authors in this study commented on previous versions of the manuscript. Also, they read and approved the final manuscript.
Written informed consent was obtained from all healthy individuals who were included in the study. Informed consent was documented in a written, signed, and dated informed consent form.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113757.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
